bio: Lydia Nora Brueggemann, later Lydia N. Stuenkel, grew up in a Lutheran minister's family that moved from Osmond to Tilden and then to Fremont, Nebraska. She graduated from Immanuel Hospital School of Nursing and worked in obstetrics at Fremont Memorial Hospital, became the first registered nurse at Hormel in Fremont, and later spent 15 years as a nurse at Arbor Manor. She was deeply involved at Trinity Lutheran Church in Fremont, teaching Sunday School for 20 years, singing in the choir, driving for Meals on Wheels, and serving as a volunteer grandma for the church day care.
funFacts: She was the fourth of seven children of Rev. William and Lydia (Noack) Brueggemann.
Her obituary says she was the first registered nurse at Hormel in Fremont.
She and Edmund H. Stuenkel Jr. married on 17 June 1952 and had three children.
Trinity Lutheran School in Fremont, which her obituary says she attended, traces its beginnings to an 1884 rented building on Main Street.
records: Dugan Funeral Chapel obituary for Lydia N. Stuenkel

citations.label: Dugan Funeral Chapel obituary: Lydia N. Stuenkel

bio: Anton Wilhelm Brueggemann was known in American records as Rev. William Brueggemann. His daughter's obituary identifies him as Rev. William Brueggemann and places the family in Osmond, Tilden, and Fremont, Nebraska between 1919 and 1925. A Find a Grave memorial identifies him as Rev. Anton Wilhelm “William” Brueggemann, son of Friedrich Wilhelm Brueggemann and Theodora Helena Lisette Weyel, and notes his marriage to Lydia E. Noack in Arlington Heights.
funFacts: His daughter Lydia's obituary says he and Lydia (Noack) Brueggemann had seven children.
A Find a Grave memorial notes that the 1920 census listed him, as William, in McCook Ward 1, Red Willow County, Nebraska, with his widowed mother.
In 1934 he conducted Emma Giesselmann’s funeral at Trinity Lutheran Church in Fremont before “a very large attendance,” according to a contemporary Arlington Review obituary transcript.
records: Dugan Funeral Chapel obituary for daughter Lydia N. Stuenkel
Washington County Genealogical Society obituary transcript for Emma Giesselmann

citations.label: Dugan Funeral Chapel obituary: Lydia N. Stuenkel
Find a Grave memorial: Rev Anton Wilhelm “William” Brueggemann
Washington County Genealogical Society obituary transcript: Emma Giesselmann

bio: Lydia Sophie Emelia Noack appears in her daughter's obituary as Lydia (Noack) Brueggemann, wife of Rev. William Brueggemann and mother of seven children. The family moved from Osmond to Tilden, Nebraska in 1921 and then to Fremont in 1925. A Find a Grave memorial for Rev. Anton Wilhelm “William” Brueggemann notes that he married Lydia E. Noack on 28 January 1912 in Arlington Heights.
funFacts: Her children included Lydia Nora, Carl, Richard, Robert, Hilda, Ruth, and Esther, as named in Lydia Nora's obituary.
records: Dugan Funeral Chapel obituary for daughter Lydia N. Stuenkel

citations.label: Dugan Funeral Chapel obituary: Lydia N. Stuenkel
Find a Grave memorial: Rev Anton Wilhelm “William” Brueggemann

bio: Friedrich Wilhelm Brueggemann is identified by Find a Grave as Pastor Friedrich Wilhelm Brueggemann. The memorial gives his birth as 24 November 1846, death as 17 May 1892, and burial at Concordia Cemetery in Forest Park, Cook County, Illinois. A Find a Grave memorial for Rev. Anton Wilhelm “William” Brueggemann identifies Friedrich Wilhelm Brueggemann and Theodora Helena Lisette Weyel as Anton's parents.
funFacts: He is buried in Concordia Cemetery in Forest Park, a major Lutheran cemetery in the Chicago area.

citations.label: Find a Grave memorial: Pastor Friedrich Wilhelm Brueggemann
Find a Grave memorial: Rev Anton Wilhelm “William” Brueggemann

bio: Charles Moritz Noack matches the C. M. Noack found in Arlington Heights Lutheran records. The Arlington Heights Herald issue of 25 February 1944 lists C. M. Noack as honorary pastor at St. Peter Lutheran Church, and the same issue's obituary for Elizabeth Schoepke says she was confirmed and later married by Rev. C. M. Noack at Arlington Heights. A Lutheran Museum article also records that Carolina Harnisch married Carl Noack of Dolton, Illinois in 1882 and identifies him as a Lutheran pastor.
funFacts: St. Peter Lutheran Church in Arlington Heights was founded in 1860 and remains a Lutheran Church–Missouri Synod congregation.
The 1944 Herald church listing names Luther V. Stephan as supply pastor and C. M. Noack as honorary pastor.
Meyers Gazetteer identifies his birthplace Wronke as a city in Kreis Samter, Province Posen, with Catholic and Protestant parish churches and a synagogue.
The Poznań Project indexes his parents’ likely 1847 marriage in the Evangelical parish at Kargowa/Karge, with Carl Wilhelm Noack aged 28 and Auguste Louise Amalie Prietzel aged 23.
records: Arlington Heights Herald, 25 February 1944 OCR text
Lutheran Heritage Center & Museum article on Adolph Harnisch
St. Peter Lutheran Church and School About page
Poznań Project marriage index for Carl Wilhelm Noack and Auguste Louise Amalie Prietzel

citations.label: St. Peter Lutheran Church and School About Us
Poznań Project marriage index: Kargowa Evangelical parish entry 49/1847

bio: Caroline Henrietta Harnisch appears in the Lutheran Museum account as Carolina Harnisch, daughter of Adolph and Christine Harnisch. The article says she married Carl Noack in St. Louis in 1882, and that Carl was from Dolton, Illinois and was a Lutheran pastor. Her family belonged to the old German Lutheran network that connected Trinity Lutheran Church in St. Louis, Concordia Seminary students, and later Lutheran pastor families.
funFacts: The article notes that Caroline's sister Elizabeth also married a Lutheran pastor, Theodore Pfotenhauer.
The author highlights Harnisch, Pfotenhauer, and Noack descendants as later Lutheran pastors.
The Lutheran Museum article reproduces the 1882 St. Louis marriage record for Carolina Harnisch and Carl Noack, identifying Carl as a Lutheran pastor from Dolton, Illinois.
records: Lutheran Heritage Center & Museum article on Adolph Harnisch

bio: Philip Anton Weyel was a Lutheran pastor and early missionary-organizer in northeastern Illinois. Immanuel Evangelical Lutheran Church in Mokena says Anton August Philip Weyel was appointed missionary for Will County, traveled on foot through the Chicago-area settlements, and helped organize German Lutheran families in Frankfort Township in 1850. St. John's Evangelical Lutheran Church, Eagle Lake says Anton Weyel was the first resident pastor of Zion in Crete and was missionary for Will County and the surrounding country, using Crete as his base. Concordia Historical Institute preserves the Philip Anton Weyel Papers, described as correspondence, manuscripts, and diplomas in German.
funFacts: Immanuel's history says Pastor Weyel traveled with a gun and his retriever, Phylax, while visiting scattered Lutheran families.
The Immanuel congregation was organized on 4 November 1850 with fifteen charter members.
Find a Grave places Rev. Phillip A. Weyel's burial at Pilgrims Rest Cemetery in Milwaukee.
Meyers Gazetteer places his birthplace Herborn in Prussian Hessen-Nassau and notes both Catholic and Protestant parish churches there.
St. John’s Eagle Lake history notes that early church-book entries from the Will County congregations were written in German, often using old-style German script.
records: Immanuel Evangelical Lutheran Church & Preschool history
St. John's Eagle Lake church history
Concordia Historical Institute Philip Anton Weyel Papers

citations.label: St. John's Evangelical Lutheran Church, Eagle Lake
Philip Anton Weyel Papers, Concordia Historical Institute
Find a Grave memorial: Rev Phillip A. Weyel

bio: Johann Adolph Harnisch was part of the Stephanite Lutheran immigration from Saxony to America. The Lutheran Museum article says he was 12 when his family sailed on the Olbers in 1838 and arrived in New Orleans with the Stephanite group in 1839; unlike many of the group, the Harnisch family stayed in St. Louis rather than moving to Perry County. He worked as a shoemaker in 1850 and 1860, later as a private watchman in 1870 and 1880, and died in 1889 while apparently living with the Dolton, Illinois household of his daughter Caroline and pastor son-in-law Carl Noack.
immigration: Sailed from Germany with the Stephanite Gesellschaft aboard the Olbers in 1838 and arrived at New Orleans in 1839; the family remained in St. Louis.
funFacts: The Olbers was also the ship on which Rev. Martin Stephan traveled.
Adolph and Christine Harnisch married at Trinity Lutheran Church in St. Louis on 28 May 1854, by Rev. Friedrich Wyneken.
The Lutheran Museum article says Adolph and Christine were buried at New Saxon Cemetery, later Concordia Cemetery, in St. Louis.
The Lutheran Museum article says the 1850 census repeated the same four-person Harnisch family seen on the Olbers passenger list and marked Adolph as a shoemaker like his father.
By 1870 he had changed trades from shoemaker to private watchman.
records: Lutheran Heritage Center & Museum article on Adolph Harnisch

bio: Christine Johanna Hagemann was born in Hohenassel, Germany and became the wife of Stephanite immigrant Adolph Harnisch. The Lutheran Museum article says Adolph Harnisch married Christine Hegemann at Trinity Lutheran Church in St. Louis on 28 May 1854, with Rev. Friedrich Wyneken officiating. The couple had six baptism records in Trinity's books, and Christine died in 1879 at age 51.
immigration: The Lutheran Museum article reports some evidence that Christine came to the United States in 1847, but the author could not locate her in the 1850 census; treat that arrival year as provisional.
funFacts: Her marriage was performed by Rev. Friedrich Wyneken, later the second president of what became the Lutheran Church–Missouri Synod.
Her daughters Elizabeth and Caroline both married Lutheran pastors.
Six Harnisch children have baptism records in Trinity Lutheran Church, St. Louis, according to the Lutheran Museum article.
The same article says their first child died as an infant before the 1860 census.
records: Lutheran Heritage Center & Museum article on Adolph Harnisch

bio: Gottfried Harnisch was the father of Johann Adolph Harnisch and one of the German Lutheran immigrants associated with the Stephanite Gesellschaft. The Lutheran Museum article says Gottfried and Friedericke Harnisch joined the group led by Rev. Martin Stephan, sailed on the Olbers, and were listed on the New Orleans passenger list with their children Johann Adolph and Anna Dorothea. The passenger-list discussion identifies Gottfried as a shoemaker from Dresden.
immigration: Sailed with the Stephanite immigration aboard the Olbers in 1838 and arrived at New Orleans in 1839; the family stayed in St. Louis.
funFacts: The article says the Harnisch surname is one of the bold-face original immigrant names in the German Family Tree used by the Lutheran Heritage Center & Museum.
In the 1860 census, the Lutheran Museum article says Gottfried’s household was listed directly above his son Adolph’s, with both men called shoemaker masters.
records: Lutheran Heritage Center & Museum article on Adolph Harnisch

bio: Johann Heinrich Christian Hagemann is identified in the Lutheran Museum article as the father of Christine Johanna Hagemann, wife of Adolph Harnisch. The article gives Christine's parents as Johann and Johanna (Stanze) Hagemann and places Christine's origins in Hohenassel, Germany. A Find a Grave memorial for Christian Johann Heinrich Hagemann gives birth on 10 June 1803, death on 17 December 1870, and burial at Saint Johns United Church of Christ Cemetery in Mehlville, St. Louis County, Missouri.
funFacts: His burial in Mehlville helps place the Hagemann-Stanze line in the St. Louis German church-record landscape.
records: Lutheran Heritage Center & Museum article on Adolph Harnisch

citations.label: Find a Grave memorial: Christian Johann Heinrich Hagemann

bio: Theodore Helene Lizetta Weyel, likely Theodora in other records, was born in Frankfort, Will County, Illinois, into the German Lutheran mission field served by her father Rev. Philip Anton Weyel. Immanuel Mokena's history says Pastor Weyel moved to the Hickory Creek settlement in 1850 and visited scattered Chicago-area Lutheran families on foot with his gun and retriever, Phylax.
funFacts: Her 1852 Will County birth came just two years after her father began organizing German Lutheran families in Frankfort Township.
Her childhood family was tied to the same mission work that organized Immanuel’s congregation on 4 November 1850 with fifteen charter members.
records: Immanuel Evangelical Lutheran Church & Preschool history

bio: Fredrick Wilhelm Gottlieb Bruggemann is listed in the tree as born at Nordholz in the former Schaumburg-Lippe area. Meyers Gazetteer identifies Nordholz as a village in Schaumburg-Lippe and points researchers to the related Berenbusch-Nordholz entry, where the court was at Bückeburg and civil registration was at Evesen.
funFacts: His stated birthplace was not a large city but a Schaumburg-Lippe village whose records lead toward Bückeburg and Evesen.

bio: Carl Wilhelm Noack and Auguste Louise Amalie Prietzel match a Poznań Project index entry for the Evangelical parish of Kargowa, entry 49 in 1847, where Carl Wilhelm Noack was recorded as age 28 and Auguste Louise Amalie Prietzel as age 23. Meyers Gazetteer lists Karge/Kargowa in Kreis Bomst, Province Posen, with its own Protestant parish church.
records: Poznań Project marriage index for Carl Wilhelm Noack and Auguste Louise Amalie Prietzel
funFacts: His 1847 marriage was indexed in the Evangelical parish at Kargowa/Karge, a Posen locality with its own Protestant parish church.

citations.label: Poznań Project marriage index: Kargowa Evangelical parish entry 49/1847

bio: Auguste Luise Amalie Prietzel matches the bride in a Poznań Project index entry for the Evangelical parish of Kargowa, entry 49 in 1847, where she married Carl Wilhelm Noack at age 23. Her stated birthplace, Grünberg in Silesia, appears in Meyers Gazetteer as a Prussian Silesian county seat in Regierungsbezirk Liegnitz.
records: Poznań Project marriage index for Carl Wilhelm Noack and Auguste Louise Amalie Prietzel
funFacts: Meyers Gazetteer describes her stated birthplace Grünberg as a Silesian county seat with two Protestant churches, one Catholic church, and a synagogue.
At her indexed 1847 marriage in Kargowa, she was recorded as 23 years old.

citations.label: Poznań Project marriage index: Kargowa Evangelical parish entry 49/1847

bio: Johann Christian Noack is associated in the tree with death at Karge in Kreis Bomst, Province Posen. Meyers Gazetteer identifies Karge as a Posen locality with its own civil registration office and one Protestant parish church, useful context for the Noack line in the Kargowa/Karge record area.
funFacts: Karge had one Protestant parish church, making it a plausible church-record center for the Noack family’s Posen records.

bio: Johanne Wilhelmine Henrietta Stanze is tied in the tree to Hohenassel in Braunschweig. Meyers Gazetteer describes Hohenassel as a village in Kreis Wolfenbüttel with its own civil registration office.
funFacts: Her Hohenassel origins point to a Braunschweig village record world rather than a large Prussian city.

bio: Ilse Dorothee Vornkahl is listed with birth at Berel and death at Hohenassel, neighboring Braunschweig villages in Kreis Wolfenbüttel. Meyers Gazetteer identifies Berel as a village with one Protestant parish church.
funFacts: Berel had its own Protestant parish church, a helpful clue for locating Vornkahl and Fasterling church records.

bio: Johann Heinrich Vornkahl is listed in the tree with birth and death at Berel in the Wolfenbüttel area. Meyers Gazetteer identifies Berel as a Braunschweig village with its own Protestant parish church.
funFacts: His life appears anchored in Berel, a Braunschweig village whose Protestant parish church is noted by Meyers Gazetteer.

bio: Ilse Elisabeth Fasterling is listed with birth and death at Berel in the Wolfenbüttel area. Meyers Gazetteer identifies Berel as a Braunschweig village with one Protestant parish church.
funFacts: Her Berel birthplace and burial place point to a small Protestant parish-village setting in Braunschweig.

bio: This roster entry appears to correspond to Hilda Lydia Brueggemann Bowers: the Pierce County 2004 obituary index lists Hilda Bowers, maiden name Brueggemann, born 11 December 1912, died 27 October 2004, with parents William Brueggemann and Lydia Noack and spouse H. Lee Bowers.
records: Pierce County, Nebraska 2004 obituary index for Hilda Bowers
funFacts: Her obituary-index entry places her burial at Grand Junction, Colorado, showing that one child of the Nebraska parsonage family later put down roots farther west.

citations.label: Pierce County, Nebraska 2004 obituary index: Hilda Bowers
1930 United States Federal Census (Ancestry.com, tree source S980815074)

bio: Ruth C. Brueggemann appears to be Ruth Clara Brueggemann Moritz, born in Osmond, Nebraska to William and Lydia (Noack) Brueggemann. Public obituary and memorial summaries place her death in 2008 at Stanton Health Center and burial at Stanton Cemetery in Stanton County, Nebraska.
records: Fremont Tribune obituary for Ruth Moritz
funFacts: She married Max L. Moritz and her later records connect the family to Stanton County, Nebraska.

citations.label: Fremont Tribune obituary via Legacy: Ruth Moritz
Find a Grave memorial: Ruth Clara Brueggemann Moritz
1930 United States Federal Census (Ancestry.com, tree source S980815074)

bio: This 1930-census son appears to be Carl W. Brueggemann, Lydia Nora Brueggemann Stuenkel’s brother Carl. Trinity Lutheran Church in El Paso, Illinois, says Pastor Carl W. Brueggemann of Gresham, Nebraska accepted the congregation’s call in 1948 and was installed on Trinity Sunday, 23 May 1948, as its first pastor.
records: Trinity Lutheran Church & Preschool, El Paso history
funFacts: At his 1948 installation as Trinity Lutheran Church of El Paso’s first pastor, the service and reception were held in the high school auditorium with nearly 400 people attending.
The new El Paso congregation rented a parsonage for him and his family at $40 per month.

citations.label: Trinity Lutheran Church & Preschool, El Paso history
1930 United States Federal Census (Ancestry.com, tree source S980815074)

bio: Esther W. Brueggemann appears to be Esther Hokamp, daughter of William and Lydia Brueggemann. Her Baue Funeral Homes obituary says she died in St. Charles, Missouri on 16 December 2010 at age 89, was the widow of Martin H. Hokamp, and had worked for many years as an administrative assistant.
records: Baue Funeral Homes obituary for Esther Hokamp
funFacts: Her obituary calls her a “G-ma, step G-ma, and Great G-ma to many.”
She belonged to Hope Lutheran Church, the Lutheran Women’s Missionary League, and other church organizations.

citations.label: Baue Funeral Homes obituary: Esther Hokamp
1930 United States Federal Census (Ancestry.com, tree source S980815074)

bio: Richard W. Brueggemann appears to be the World War II soldier of that name from Nebraska. HonorStates and memorial summaries identify him as a U.S. Army Private First Class who died on 27 October 1944 at age 19 in the Philippines and is buried at Manila American Cemetery and Memorial.
records: HonorStates World War II casualty profile for Richard W. Brueggemann
funFacts: He died one week after the 96th Infantry Division entered combat on Leyte on 20 October 1944.
His burial is reported at Manila American Cemetery and Memorial, Plot L, Row 11, Grave 137.

citations.label: HonorStates: Richard W. Brueggemann, World War II casualty
Find a Grave memorial: PFC Richard W. Brueggemann
1930 United States Federal Census (Ancestry.com, tree source S980815074)

bio: Henning Andreas Vornkahl is listed in the tree with birth and death at Berel in the Wolfenbüttel area of Braunschweig. Meyers Gazetteer identifies Berel as a village in Kreis Wolfenbüttel with its own later civil registration office and one Protestant parish church. Treat the Berel details here as place context for the locality named in the tree, with the Protestant parish being the more relevant clue for eighteenth-century events.
funFacts: Berel had its own Protestant parish church, a useful clue for researching Vornkahl church-book entries.

bio: A GEDBAS profile for Heinrich Georg Vornkahl gives his birth as 7 June 1683 and death as 8 January 1753. The tree places those events at Berel, and Meyers Gazetteer identifies Berel as a Braunschweig village in Kreis Wolfenbüttel with one Protestant parish church and its own later civil registration office. The GEDBAS entry notes that its submitter does not display source references, so the profile should be treated as a clue rather than a fully sourced proof.
records: GEDBAS profile for Heinrich Georg Vornkahl
funFacts: His GEDBAS dates match the tree dates closely, but the public GEDBAS page withholds the underlying source citations.

bio: Michael Vornkahl is listed in the tree with birth and death at Berel in Braunschweig. Meyers Gazetteer identifies Berel as a village in Kreis Wolfenbüttel with one Protestant parish church and its own later civil registration office. Because no independent online person record was found during this pass, this entry limits itself to place context for the Berel locality named in the tree.
funFacts: Berel belonged to the Braunschweig rather than Prussian administrative record world in Meyers Gazetteer.

bio: Annecken Dieckmann is listed in the tree with origins at Lesse and death at Berel, two Braunschweig villages in Kreis Wolfenbüttel. Meyers Gazetteer identifies both Lesse and Berel as villages with Protestant parish churches and later civil registration offices. This is village context for the places attached to her tree events, not an independent online confirmation of her personal dates.
funFacts: Lesse and Berel were both in Kreis Wolfenbüttel and both pointed to the Amtsgericht at Salder in Meyers Gazetteer.

bio: Ilse geb. Büring is listed in the tree with birth in Berel and death at Berel. Meyers Gazetteer identifies Berel as a Braunschweig village in Kreis Wolfenbüttel with one Protestant parish church and its own later civil registration office. The German “geb.” in the recorded name indicates a maiden surname, so the entry preserves the tree’s naming without expanding it beyond the sourced place context.
funFacts: Her recorded Berel-to-Berel life events point to a small-village Braunschweig parish setting.

bio: Ilse Catharine BÜHRIG is listed in the tree with birth at Groß Himstedt and as part of the Vornkahl line connected to Berel. Meyers Gazetteer identifies Gross Himstedt as a village in Prussian Hannover, Regierungsbezirk Hildesheim, Kreis Marienburg, with one Protestant parish church and its own later civil registration office. The nearby Berel context comes from the tree relationship, while the cited source is used only for the Gross Himstedt locality.
records: Meyers Gazetteer entry for Gross Himstedt
Meyers Gazetteer combined entry for Gross and Klein Himstedt
funFacts: Meyers Gazetteer has a combined Himstedt entry that redirects researchers to separate Gross Himstedt and Klein Himstedt place pages.

bio: Johann Heinrich Fasterling is listed in the tree in connection with Berel in Braunschweig. Meyers Gazetteer identifies Berel as a village in Kreis Wolfenbüttel with one Protestant parish church and its own later civil registration office. No stronger independent person-level source was found in this pass, so the enrichment is limited to the Berel village context attached to the tree entry.
funFacts: The Berel Protestant parish is the likely locality to investigate for Fasterling family church records named in the tree.
